speaker: interviewer
text: [? Thank ?] [? you. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so there were seven of you, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And now we're boarding another train, and we are going in the direction of the Romanian border. And we were getting to another [? rather ?] known Jewish town in Poland, at first Stanislau, Stanyslaviv. Today it is called Ivano-Frankivsk by the Ukrainians. But then we got into war, where, where we had-- food is always, when you run away, an important thing. And so in Stanislau, there were no bombs yet. In [? Lviv, ?] they were constantly bombing us. In Stanislau, when we arrived, there were no bombs yet. And so we had breakfast. And by the time we finished breakfast, the bombers were coming. And so we got on another train, and we went to a town called Kolomyja, K-O-L-O-M-Y-J-A. Now, from there, it's about walking distance to the Romanian border, where we met one of the [? Katowice ?] rabbis, who my parents knew. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He later on was a military rabbi, at one point, is Israel. At one time, he was a rabbi in the suburb of [? Haifa ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Anyhow, he survived. And he was living there with his in-laws. And so they were all together, and they were making an [? assifi-- ?] what is called in Yiddish. It is a meeting, and we're trying to decide what to do. And some went, let's go to Romania, and let's not go to Romania. And so no decision, really, was made. But a very nice couple, a lawyer named Kramer, took us in for the night. It was on Friday evening, so the [? rabbuna ?] were busy getting ready for shabbos, so there wasn't much time to talk to us. I guess, for the first time-- it was one week after we had started off, because the war started on Friday. September 1 was a Friday. In other words, next Friday. And we finally could take a bath. We slept in a bed. And that was like heaven for a moment. And in Kolomyja, they were not bombing. But the next day, there was no use to stay there. And my father had doubts. My father, at that-- in 1939 was 47 years old. By Polish law, he was still obligated to military service.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was afraid, if we go out of Poland-- and at that time, there were rumors that [INAUDIBLE], the German advance had been stopped by the Poles-- that if we get out and the war comes to an end, he can be considered a deserter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, this is what I call-- excuse me for the expression-- the bloody Prussian upbringing-- that respect for authority and that fear of doing something which is not quite legal. It is, oftentimes, in life, a hindrance-- as much as it helps in some ways, but it is often a hindrance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were on a train. And we were, passed on the train, through another famous place in-- who knows literature and has seen the movie with Danny Kaye, Horodenka. Because that man, the Jew in that movie that Danny Kaye represents allegedly is from Horodenka.

speaker: interviewee
text: We passed through Horodenka, and then the train goes into Romania. Romanian soldiers are standing on the running boards, because nobody can go in and out. You don't need a passport. The train runs for about three kilometers, or three or four miles, through Romania. There's no customs or nothing. And then on the other side, they jump off the running boards, and you are back in Poland. And you come to a station called [INAUDIBLE], and this is where it was a spa. And that's where you can cross into Romania. But my father decided no. And the other seven-- the other five goes, decided no, also. And so we stay on the train, and we continue to travel. And now, for the first time, a conductor comes in, and he demands to sell us tickets. We have been travelling since [INAUDIBLE] without a ticket. And here a nut is suddenly-- [LAUGHS] He wants tickets. I-- he pocketed some money for himself. There was nobody there anymore to collect it from him. And so we got into a town called Tarnopol, T-A-R-N-O-P-O-L. I knew I had acquaintances there. I had spent the summer of 1936 on a Jewish farm, as I had mentioned, that we wanted to go there. I had fallen in love with the daughter of the administrator. I had corresponded with her. And two of her brethren were working in Tarnopol-- one was a beer brewer, brewmaster, and the other one was an assistant. We said, we are going to go and see them. We went to see them, and they said, we are packing our things. We are going to Russia. We are not waiting for the Germans. I think the younger one of them had a little bit of Communist interest before already. But in order to get out from there, we also needed a permit from the county. This time we went. We went to the county, and we got on the train in the evening and came to a town called Zbarazh. By then, we had heard the rumors that, from the city, [INAUDIBLE], where my mother was, they had evacuated people in this direction of the Lithuanian border, of Wilno. So now we're not trying anymore to go there. We're trying to go in the direction of Wilno. And our schedule, our map, shows us that from Zbarazh, there is no train. But there is a town, again, known in Polish, [INAUDIBLE] from there, there is a train connection in the direction of Wilno.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the seven of us hire two farmers-- unbeknownst to us, Ukrainians. And they agreed, for a lot of money, to take us to Zbarazh. That was one nightmarish ride at night in the dark, with people whom we didn't know, where we didn't know that there were Ukrainian bands in the forest over there, and where the roads were not roads. This is a piece of dirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it rains, you probably can't drive through. That's like something like people were going in the Wild West in America years ago. At one point at night, the horses stopped. There must've been some bridge, or something, a little stream. In any case, we got into [INAUDIBLE] in the wee morning hours. And here suddenly, they dropped us at the edge of the town and went back. Luckily, they neither robbed nor killed us. And then there is a stream of vehicles, civilian and military, going through, which later out, we found out was the Polish government running, and the general's staff running away to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you this question? I know eventually you fall into the Russian hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it that you run into the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, in [INAUDIBLE], we went, we went to sleep in the evening. And before we went to sleep, a military motorcycle had stopped and called my father's name-- the guy who was riding it. And he said he was the son of one of my grandfather's-- my father's former partners.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a reserve officer. And he said, Dr. Guttmann, the Russians are coming. And my father said, are they going with us or against us? He said, that, we don't know yet. So we went to sleep, and in the morning, we heard that the Russians had come, but we didn't see any. Well, that was at that place, and that lieutenant had told us, get out from here, and see that you get into a big town, because in the small places, it's going to be terrible. So we first went back to Zbarazh. We hired another farmer, this time only one. We all squeezed into one wagon. Money was running short. We went back to Zbar-- to [INAUDIBLE] on the border-- not Zbarazh, [INAUDIBLE]. We went back to [INAUDIBLE], stayed in [INAUDIBLE] eight days, until the first train wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: There, one day, a troop, a group of Russians came on a truck. They were the first Russians we saw. It turned out, they were from the fire department, the reason being that the fire department in Russia is part of the Ministry of the Interior. Now, the Ministry of the Interior doesn't take care of forests, but this is the Ministry of the Police. And the fire department was subordinated to the police, because when there is a fire, there is suspicions that it is sabotage. So that were the first Russians we saw. When a train came, finally, it consisted of one freight car, a wood-fired locomotive, a-- two people, two engineers on the locomotive, and an officer from the tank trucks, tank-- our tank, some tank groups-- you could see by his insignia-- as the escort. And that was the first military Soviet one we saw. And then we went back into Tarnopol, and were first housed by some Jews who had-- there were immediately lots of organizations who were trying to help. The first couple of nights, we slept in an abandoned apartment on straw. But then we found some people, and some people were sending us to other people, and we were housed here and there. And suddenly we met more people from our city. And I went back to the brewery and found out that my girlfriend had arrived there with her parents. They were, had been running away from the farm, too, but they had horses, so they went with the horses with the wagon. And I got a job in the brewery schlepping sacks with barley and, and, and, and malt. My father subsequently got a job with the shoemakers as an accountant. And then we rented a room. But in the meantime, one more thing happened. One day, a lot of men from Katowice arrived. They explained to us that they had gotten a summons from the Nazis to appear on a certain day at the sports palace in the city and had been given a list what to bring with them-- and there were how many pairs of underwear, socks, so on, so on, an ax if they have one, a spade, tools, anything, because they are going to be sent to work somewhere. What they did, they drove them to a station called-- a rail station called [INAUDIBLE]. That's not far from where our train abandoned us. And they were told, get out, and they were pointed in that direction. They said, this is where the Russian border is, because in the meantime, the Russians had occupied part of Poland. They will come, had come to the [INAUDIBLE]. They said, you go over there. You've got 24 hours to disappear. And at the station, some farmers were waiting with wagons, and offered the people to carry the luggage. And some were dumb enough to give them their luggage, and the farmers disappeared with their luggage-- barely, it was on the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me ask you this. You were not with these men, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this has been relayed to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met them, and they came, but some of them were friends of my father's?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So they eventually show up here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they told us about how that whole procedure went.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. If we can get back to the story that you saw, that you witnessed. At some point, you fall into-- the Russians offer you an ultimatum, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: In spring--

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to get to that part of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: In spring of 19-- 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, spring of 1940. It could have been still winter. It was a terrible winter, cold. All the refugees-- by then they had taken care of various other people, sent them to Siberia. Now, they were taking on the refugees. There were, in the city, about what-- the last few thousand. They were called to the-- called to appear at certain dates to the [INAUDIBLE]-- police, militia, and [INAUDIBLE]-- all in the same building. And they only work in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are interviewed, being interviewed by a colonel, who gives us, after filling out some papers with all the questions, personal data, said, what is your decision? You've got two choices. You can accept Soviet citizenship, or you can go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had made an app-- since we had, he had a good job by then, had made an application to the Soviets. And we had found out where my mother is and my sisters, [INAUDIBLE]. Which they had to determine. Meantime, they were in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: My father filed an application with a certificate that he had a job to permit my mother and my sister to join us in Tarnopol. So he told the guy, the colonel, what about my wife-- can she-- and my sister, my daughter? Can they join me here? And at that point, the colonel said-- and that, I am a witness to, what he said. I mean, I may not need the words exactly, but the gist of it is-- what do you need your wife for? Get yourself another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that point, my father said, we are going back to Poland. Now, there were rumors that there  is going to be a committee which will review those repatriation requests, and so on. And in Lemberg, they allegedly had seen, already, Germans, so it was done. Because my father said to me the most important thing is the family together. It never came to it. On June 27, 1940, at-- after midnight, two men in what I called civilian uniforms-- which means they were not really uniforms. They were high boots, black riding britches, and black-- those Russian [INAUDIBLE]-- knocked at the door, came in and said, Guttmann, Fritz, [? Bernard Goldreich, ?] and Guttmann, Max, [INAUDIBLE], [? Fritz Goldreich. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? We had our papers, all the forms. Pack your things. You are going back to Poland. Pack everything. Don't leave anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to make a long story short, there was a truck waiting. Our landlords were able to give us a little bit food on the way. There was a truck standing in front of the building. They drove us to the railroad station. They loaded us on, subsequently, in due time, on freight cars, 40 people to a freight car. And for two days, we were standing on that station. For the first 12 or more hours, we didn't even get a bucket of water to drink. There was one baby in our compartment which was being still nourished by its mother. They allowed some people from the city, from my father's company, they came, and-- with the payroll, and my father got some money. And some other people got some money. They allowed that. But otherwise, from that one side of the train, they were checking who was-- and they were allowing a few people. On the other side of the train, soldiers with carbines and bayonets on the carbines, were patrolling, going forward and back, which did not hinder certain people from the city to come in. And when the soldier was going this way, they [? snuck ?] to somebody here. And when they come the other way, they knew they wouldn't shoot. I mean, that cruel, they hadn't been yet at that time, at least. And so a girlfriend I had met over there came to me three times. She brought me food, she brought me something to drink, because we were thirsty. It was hot. She climbed over fences. She got in between freight trains, under the trains, and so on, and other people also were getting stuff there. But the next night, a [? rainy ?] night, suddenly the doors opened, and two officers came. And he said, [INAUDIBLE]. That means bachelors-- bachelors or bachelorettes-- out. They considered a bachelor, at the time, anybody who was not with a family, a wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father said, we are not bachelors. We are family together. And so did a couple of our other friends who were brothers or so. From our car, nobody left. But some people did. And for-- until 1943, November, 1943, I didn't know what happened to them, because some of the people were our friends who left there. They were taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then about 24-- no, 36 hours after we had embarked on the train, the train moved. And the moment the train moved, we knew we were not going to Poland. We knew we were going to Russia, because one-- of the direction. But besides, we stopped at a station, which again, in German literature is known, [INAUDIBLE]. There was a writer, [INAUDIBLE], who wrote a story about that. And I was not-- knew about that. I knew that story. So we were at [INAUDIBLE], and we went into Russia. We were on the train about probably 10 days, 12 days. I don't know exactly. We went to Kiev. We were in [INAUDIBLE]. We were in [INAUDIBLE]. We were in [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you, eventually, where did you eventually end?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ended in, on the rail, on the railroad line-- on a sideline of the rail line which goes from [INAUDIBLE] to Vladivostok, past the line separating Europe from Si-- from Asia, in Siberia. And the district, or as the [INAUDIBLE], the Russians call it Oblast of Tyuman, which is T-Y-U-M-A-N, County [INAUDIBLE], sector 248 of the [INAUDIBLE] Forest Industry. That was one at the beginning, one-- what do you call-- block, round, buildings-- a log cabin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's fine. It's fine. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: One huge log cabin divided in several--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --parts on the one side of the railroad tracks, a more modern barracks building on the other side of the railroad tracks, and a couple single houses about two or three miles away-- all together about 1,000 people in that settlement.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you arrive in Siberia. When do you arrive in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: First days of July-- 10th, 15th, about between 10th and 15th of July, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You arrive in Siberia, and what do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same evening, we were assigned places. And all those unattached men got one big room. There were about 25 of us. And we got assigned-- they had made-- there were no beds in the beginning. There were two rows of, like, bunks. And you got assigned [INAUDIBLE] on your bunk, and then you got called outside, and there was a commandant who introduced himself as his name being Commandant [INAUDIBLE] in a field uniform. And he told us, now, you are here as special re-settlers-- [RUSSIAN], in Russian, they called it. And you are going to live here until, he said, when hair is growing on my hand. That's when you're going to go back to Poland. You're going to live here. You're going to die here. In the meantime, the mosquitoes were biting like hell. And he said, [RUSSIAN]. [RUSSIAN] means never mind. You'll get used to it. He said, you're going to work in the forest. Tomorrow morning, you're going to get your tools, and we're going to assign you to your jobs. In the morning, came a man who introduced himself as a foreman, [INAUDIBLE]. He told us, still-- the commandant, you have no political rights, which means you must address all the Russians, here, all the Soviet citizens, as [RUSSIAN], as Citizen So and So, or Citizen So and So. You're not allowed to call them comrade. You are not allowed to elect your spokesman. I'm going to assign a spokesman for every one of the halls in which you live. You have no right to elect anybody or anything. Your mail is going to be delivered through our control. But you have full economical rights, which means you have to get paid the same as Soviet citizens are getting paid for their jobs, with the provision that 10% of your earnings are going to be deducted to keep for the [INAUDIBLE], for apparently, he didn't explain us, for supervising us, for taking care of us. The next morning, the foreman came. [INAUDIBLE], Citizen [INAUDIBLE]. And some of us got assigned to a sawmill, and some got assigned to the forest. My father and I, we got two axes and a saw and a bottle of kerosene and a rag and-- on a stick. And we were led to the forest, and we were shown how to cut down trees. And if, say, a bottle of kerosene was needed because in summertime, the resin sticks to the saw, so you moisten the saw a little bit with resin, and we were told what to do and how many cubic meters of what we had to produce every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for the Russians like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we were so good at lumberjacking that from that job we got transferred, because we would have gone through the forest in no time. [LAUGHS] And we got assigned to sawmill, which was a little better. And then I got assigned to various jobs, and subsequently my father to other jobs too. But we stayed there until almost September of 1941. When the war had broken out with-- between Russia and Germany-- first of all, all mail stopped. From that day on-- until then, we had been getting mail. We even got a parcel from my mother from Poland, from German-occupied Poland. But the day the war started, no mail from the West was arriving anymore. From East, we got some mail later. So in August of '41, apparently, the Polish exile government in London signed a treaty with the Russians, with the Soviets, which provided for a number of things-- the creation of a Polish army in Russia, [INAUDIBLE], which was supposed to fight with the Russians. The release of those people who had been at night, in June of 1940, sent to Siberia, and other people who had been earlier sent to Siberia, who were Polish citizens-- but who were Polish citizens, they made a point. There were a couple of people there who claimed they were Czechs and English and other things, and where by coincidence, they wouldn't be released at the time. We allegedly were allowed to go to wherever we wanted. We were supposed to get temporary documents stating that we were Poles, which in, which, which was, in a given time, had to be exchanged for Polish passports, the problem being that no consulate agreement had been signed, and there was no place to really exchange those, it turned out later. There were also restrictions.
